title: AFCAT 2026 — Air Force Common Admission Test
meta_description: AFCAT 2026 — become an Air Force officer. Flying/Technical/Ground Duty branches. Exam pattern, salary ₹56K+, flying allowance, and SSB interview.

hero.one_liner: Officer entry into Indian Air Force through AFCAT — Flying Branch (pilots), Technical Branch (engineers), Ground Duty Branch (admin/logistics).

what_is_it: AFCAT (Air Force Common Admission Test) is the official entrance exam for recruiting officers in the Indian Air Force (IAF). Conducted twice yearly (typically February and August), AFCAT offers pathways to three distinct officer branches: Flying Branch (to become fighter/transport/helicopter pilots), Technical Branch (for engineering officers managing aircraft systems and maintenance), and Ground Duty Branch (for administrative, logistics, accounts, education, and meteorology officers). AFCAT is the primary route for officer entry post-graduation — alternative routes include NDA (after 12th) and CDS (combined defense services).

The AFCAT selection process involves two major stages: (1) Written Test: 100 MCQs in 2 hours testing General Awareness, Verbal Ability, Numerical Ability, and Reasoning/Military Aptitude. This is a screening test with moderate difficulty. (2) AFSB (Air Force Selection Board): A 5-day intense process conducted at Air Force stations including psychological tests, group discussions, interviews, and medical examination. Flying candidates additionally undergo PABT (Pilot Aptitude Battery Test) and medical exam at Air Force Center of Medical Examination. The entire selection is merit-based with no preference for family background or connections.

Starting salary as a Flying Officer is at Level 10 of Indian pay scale (₹56,100 basic) plus Flying Allowance (₹25,000/month for pilots) and other allowances, totaling approximately ₹90,000-1,20,000/month for pilots. Technical and Ground Duty officers earn ₹80,000-95,000/month. The glamour of the Air Force uniform, the thrill of flying fighter jets (for pilots), the security of government service, and rapid promotions make AFCAT highly attractive. Many AFCAT aspirants are driven by childhood dreams of flying, combined with job security and societal respect.

AFCAT 2026 has two cycles: AFCAT 1 (conducted January 31, 2026 — now completed) and AFCAT 2 (expected August 2026, with notification likely in June). Candidates can appear for multiple AFCAT cycles and can apply for multiple branches simultaneously (Flying + Technical + Ground Duty). If selected for Flying and not found suitable during training, candidates may be offered Technical or Ground Duty options, allowing career flexibility.

key_details.label: Flying Branch Age
key_details.value: 20-24 years. Graduate + Physics & Mathematics at 12th level. 60% marks required.

key_details.label: Ground Duty Age
key_details.value: 20-26 years. Graduate in any stream. MBA/MCA/MA/MSc for specific posts. 60% required.

key_details.label: Selection Stages
key_details.value: Written Test → AFSB Interview (5 days) → Medical Exam → Final Merit

key_details.label: Service Type
key_details.value: Short Service Commission (14 years) or Permanent Commission (till retirement at 60)

key_details.label: PABT
key_details.value: Pilot Aptitude Battery Test — unique for Flying Branch candidates

key_details.label: AFCAT Frequency
key_details.value: Twice per year: February and August cycles

exam_pattern.description: 100 MCQs distributed across 4 sections. Online exam at designated IAF exam centers. Moderate difficulty compared to CDS/NDA. Focus on speed and accuracy.
exam_pattern.negative_marking: -1 mark deducted per wrong answer. Each question carries 3 marks. +3 for correct, 0 for unattempted.

major_posts.department: Indian Air Force — Ground Duty Branch

extra_sections.heading: Three AFCAT Branches — Selection Criteria & Lifestyle
rows: High physical demand, frequent transfers, thrill & risk
Office-based initially, field postings, technical work
Any graduate, MBA/MCA for specific posts, age 20-26

extra_sections.heading: AFCAT Written vs AFSB — Equal Importance
content:
Written Test — Screening Only

AFCAT written test is moderate difficulty (easier than NDA, comparable to CDS). It's primarily a screening exam — clearing cutoff (typically 40-50% for top candidates) gets you AFSB call.
Once you're in AFSB, written test score carries minimal weightage (10-15%). This means: Your written score is important to qualify for AFSB, but once at AFSB, interview performance matters much more.

AFSB — Personality & Officer Quality Assessment

The 5-day AFSB process is where actual selection happens. Components: (1) Group discussions on current affairs and defense topics, (2) Psychological tests (FSOT, WAT, TAT), (3) Personal interview (15-20 minutes), (4) Medical examination.
Each component is scored. AFSB assesses: Officer Like Qualities (OLQs) — leadership, decision-making, temperament, patriotism, adaptability.
A brilliant written test scorer can fail AFSB if they lack leadership or communication skills. Conversely, average written scorers with strong personality and communication can crack AFSB.

extra_sections.heading: Flying Branch vs Other Branches — Career Trajectory
rows: Fast — from Flying Officer to Group Captain in 20 years
Air Force bases across India + possibly Siachen/high altitude
Very high — ejection risk, G-forces, high stress
Pilot training instructor, defense contractor (high pay)

preparation_tips: AFCAT written test is moderate difficulty — easier than NDA/CDS. Focus on: English grammar and vocabulary (25 marks), current affairs with defense focus (25 marks), basic mathematics (18 marks), and reasoning with spatial/military aptitude (32 marks). Don't over-prepare — spend 2-3 months on AFCAT written, then shift focus to AFSB preparation which is more important.

Military Aptitude & Reasoning questions are unique to AFCAT — spatial reasoning (3D rotations, perspective views), direction sense, and group judgment scenarios. Practice with AFCAT-specific mock tests. These questions don't appear in other exams like CAT/IBPS. Standard competitive exam practice won't help here — AFCAT mocks are essential.

Current affairs with defense angle is heavily tested. Follow: Defense Ministry announcements, Indian Air Force press releases, defense capabilities of India and neighboring countries, major defense projects (Tejas, Akash, etc.), and global military developments. Read Indian Express defense section weekly. In interview, defense knowledge is crucial — being unaware of India's latest fighter jets or missile systems is a red flag.

AFSB preparation (5-day board) is where real selection happens. Prepare: Officer Like Qualities (decision-making, leadership, maturity), group discussion skills, current affairs depth, physical fitness (AFSB has PE tests), and confidence. Many written test scorers fail AFSB due to poor communication or lack of presence. Join AFSB-specific coaching 1-2 months before board for guidance on group discussions, interview technique, and psychological test familiarization.

For Flying Branch: PABT (Pilot Aptitude Battery) is a separate test conducted at Air Force Center. It tests hand-eye coordination, spatial awareness, and instrument comprehension. If you fail PABT even once, you're permanently ineligible for Flying Branch. Prepare by: Flight simulator games, 3D visualization puzzles, direction-finding exercises. This is a make-or-break component for pilots.

faqs.question: Can I become a fighter pilot through AFCAT?
faqs.answer: Yes. AFCAT Flying Branch is one primary pathway to becoming a fighter pilot. After selection, you undergo 74 weeks of training at Air Force Academy, Dundigal. Post-graduation, you receive initial flying training (ab initio), then advanced training. Fighter/Transport/Helicopter allocation depends on your training performance — top performers are typically allocated to fighters. Another path: NDA (after 12th) is more prestigious for pilot entry but requires appearing at 17. AFCAT is the post-graduation alternative.

faqs.question: AFCAT vs NDA vs CDS for Air Force entry?
faqs.answer: Three paths: (1) NDA — After 12th (age 16.5-19.5), 3 years NDA + 1 year AFA training. Entry at 22 as commissioned officer. Most prestigious. (2) CDS — After graduation (age 20-24 for Flying), 74 weeks AFA training. Entry at 22-25. (3) AFCAT — After graduation (age 20-24 for Flying), same 74 weeks AFA training. NDA is the premium choice (top students). CDS and AFCAT are equivalent post-graduation options. NDA has higher competition but prestigious. Choose based on: If you can appear at 17, do NDA. If graduating, choose AFCAT.

faqs.question: I wear glasses. Can I apply for AFCAT Flying Branch?
faqs.answer: Flying Branch has strict vision requirement: 6/6 in each eye WITHOUT glasses/contacts. If your vision is below 6/6 uncorrected, you're not eligible for Flying. However, PRK/LASIK refractive surgery is now accepted with conditions: (1) At least 1 year post-surgery, (2) Vision stable at 6/6 uncorrected, (3) No regression, (4) No complications. Technical and Ground Duty have relaxed vision standards (corrected vision 6/9 acceptable). Many gifted students with glasses pursue Technical or Ground Duty instead of Flying.

faqs.question: What's the salary difference between Flying and Ground Duty?
faqs.answer: Basic pay (Level 10) is identical. The difference is Flying Allowance: Pilots get ₹25,000/month extra. Over a 14-year Short Service Commission (SSC), this is ₹25K × 12 × 14 = ₹42 lakh in additional earnings. Pilots also get faster promotions and prestige. However, pilots face higher risk and extreme physical demands. Ground Duty offers stability, less stress, and still respectable salary. Choose based on: Risk appetite, physical fitness, career goals, and family considerations.

faqs.question: I scored well in AFCAT written but failed AFSB. Can I retry?
faqs.answer: Yes. AFSB rejection is not permanent. You can appear for the next AFCAT cycle (6 months later) and attempt AFSB again. Many candidates crack AFSB on 2nd or 3rd attempt after improving communication, group discussion skills, and current affairs knowledge. Your written test score doesn't carry over — each AFCAT cycle is independent. Use AFSB feedback to identify weak areas (leadership? Communication? Confidence?) and work specifically on them.

syllabus.subject: General Awareness
syllabus.topics: History, geography, polity, economics, current affairs, sports, awards, defence-related GK, basic science, Indian Air Force knowledge

syllabus.subject: Verbal Ability in English
syllabus.topics: Comprehension, error detection, sentence completion, synonym/antonym, testing of vocabulary, idioms & phrases

syllabus.subject: Numerical Ability
syllabus.topics: Decimal & fractions, percentage, average, ratio, profit & loss, SI/CI, time & distance, time & work, number system & series

syllabus.subject: Reasoning & Military Aptitude
syllabus.topics: Verbal & non-verbal reasoning, sequence & series, coding-decoding, analogy, direction, pattern completion, spatial ability

study_plan.tasks: Study all 4 sections from Arihant AFCAT guide. Learn about IAF — aircraft (Rafale, Sukhoi, Tejas), commands, air bases, ranks, recent operations. English vocabulary 20 words/day. Basic maths practice.
Solve previous AFCAT papers. If applying for technical branch, prepare for Engineering Knowledge Test (EKT) — branch-specific questions. Current affairs focusing on defence news. Mock tests weekly.

If written exam clears, prepare for Air Force Selection Board (AFSB) — similar to SSB. Psychological tests, group tasks, personal interview. Stay physically fit. Know why you want to join IAF — genuine motivation matters.
